items.title: The Biggest EV Mistake South Africans Make Before Buying
items.summary: Most people start in the same place when they look at an electric vehicle (EV). They compare range. They look at charging speed. They check the price. That all makes sense. It feels like the…
items.content_html:
Most people start in the same place when they look at an electric vehicle (EV).

They compare range.
They look at charging speed.
They check the price.

That all makes sense. It feels like the right way to choose a car.

But there’s one question that matters far more than any of those:

Where will you charge most of the time?

Why this matters more than anything else

An EV fits into your life differently from a petrol or diesel car.

With a petrol car, you drive until you need fuel, then you stop and fill up. It’s a separate activity.

With an EV, charging becomes part of your routine. It works best when the car charges while it is already parked.

That usually means:

  • At home overnight
  • At work during the day
  • At places you visit regularly

If you get this part right, owning an EV feels simple. If you get it wrong, it can feel like a constant effort.

The mistake most buyers make

Many first-time buyers focus on public charging.

It’s easy to see why. Public chargers are visible. You notice them at shopping centres, along highways, and in parking areas. They feel like the main solution.

In practice, they are only part of the picture.

Public charging is useful for:

  • Long trips
  • Occasional top-ups
  • Situations where you have no other option

It is not where most people should be doing the majority of their charging.

What ownership actually looks like

For most EV owners in South Africa, daily charging happens in quiet moments.

The car stands overnight at home.
It sits in a parking bay at work.
It waits while you go about your day.

Those are the moments where charging fits naturally.

If your setup allows for this, you stop thinking about charging altogether. It becomes something that happens in the background.

The South African reality

This is where things become more specific.

Your living situation plays a big role.

  • If you have a garage or a dedicated parking space, home charging is usually straightforward.
  • If you live in an estate or complex, you may need permission or adjustments.
  • If you rely on street parking, things become more complicated.

Electricity supply also matters. Load shedding, solar setups, and backup systems can all affect how and when you charge.

These are everyday realities in South Africa. They shape the ownership experience more than any spec sheet.

A better way to think before you buy

Before choosing an EV, step back from the numbers for a moment.

Ask yourself:

  • Where does my car spend most of its time parked?
  • Is there a practical way to charge it there?
  • How often do I drive long distances?
  • What will I do if my usual charging option is unavailable?

These answers will tell you far more than range figures alone.

What people often realise later

Many new owners only discover this after the car arrives.

They find that daily driving is easy. Short trips and commuting work well. The car feels smooth and convenient.

Then they start to notice the gaps:

  • Charging takes planning in certain situations
  • Public chargers are not always available
  • Some locations require apps or setup in advance

None of this makes EV ownership difficult. It just means the setup matters from the start.

The simple takeaway

Buying an EV is not only about choosing the right car.

It is about choosing a setup that fits your life.

If your home or workplace supports charging, the experience becomes straightforward.
If it doesn’t, you will rely more on workarounds.

That one detail shapes everything that follows.

In the next article, we’ll look at where South Africans actually charge their EVs day to day, and how different setups change the ownership experience.

items.title: Dongfeng Nammi 06 EV Arrives in SA
items.summary: Dongfeng has introduced its latest electric vehicle to the South African market with the arrival of the Nammi 06, a compact crossover SUV that undercuts rivals by a significant margin. Priced from R499,900, the Nammi…
items.content_html:
Dongfeng has introduced its latest electric vehicle to the South African market with the arrival of the Nammi 06, a compact crossover SUV that undercuts rivals by a significant margin.

Priced from R499,900, the Nammi 06 is now the country’s most affordable fully electric SUV, undercutting previous entry-level models such as the BYD Atto 3 and Geely E5 by approximately R200,000.

This marks the third model from the state-owned Chinese manufacturer to reach local shores following the Box hatchback and 007 sedan, which launched in late 2025.

Distributed by E Auto Motor, the Nammi 06 is being dispatched to dealerships in Boksburg, Pretoria, Cape Town, and Durban, where test drives are already available.

The move reflects the accelerating influx of affordable Chinese EVs into South Africa, driven by growing demand for sustainable transport amid rising fuel costs and environmental concerns.

The Nammi 06 is offered in two variants: the E1 and the range-topping E3. Both are powered by a single front-mounted permanent magnet synchronous electric motor producing 120kW and 230Nm of torque.

This setup enables a 0-100km/h sprint in 7.8 seconds and a top speed of 150km/h. The entry-level E1 features a 44.94kWh lithium iron phosphate (LFP) battery, delivering a claimed 401km range under the Chinese Light-Duty Vehicle Test Cycle (CLTC).

Real-world expectations under the more stringent Worldwide Harmonised Light Vehicle Test Procedure (WLTP) are closer to 300km. DC fast charging from 30 to 80 percent takes around 30 minutes.

The E3 upgrades to a larger 51.87kWh battery for a CLTC range of 471km (approximately 350km WLTP), with faster DC charging capability of up to 167kW, achieving 30-80 percent in 18 minutes.

Both models support vehicle-to-load (V2L) functionality, making them practical for outdoor use or power outages. Styling follows Dongfeng’s modern EV design language, with sleek lines, LED lighting, roof rails, and a practical 500-litre boot accessible via a split-opening tailgate.

The E1 rides on 17-inch alloy wheels, while the E3 gains 18-inch rims, a panoramic sunroof, and heated mirrors.

Inside, both offer leatherette upholstery, a multifunction steering wheel, dual digital displays (8.8-inch driver’s instrument cluster and 12.8-inch touchscreen infotainment), wireless smartphone charging, and a six-speaker audio system.

Higher trims add ambient lighting, ventilated and heated driver’s seat with memory function, and advanced driver assistance systems including adaptive cruise control, 360-degree camera, lane-keep assist, and automatic emergency braking.

Safety equipment is comprehensive across the range, with six airbags, electronic stability control, hill-start assist, tyre-pressure monitoring, and multiple active safety features.

All models come with a five-year/100,000km vehicle warranty, five-year/100,000km service plan, and eight-year/200,000km battery warranty.

The Nammi 06’s competitive pricing and feature set position it strongly against established players in South Africa’s burgeoning EV segment.

As infrastructure improves and government incentives evolve, models like this could accelerate the transition to electric mobility for everyday South African motorists.

Industry observers will watch closely to see whether this latest Dongfeng offering can capture meaningful market share in a field increasingly crowded with Chinese brands.

items.title: OMODA launches C5 SHS hybrid
items.summary: OMODA South Africa has added the C5 SHS to its local vehicle range. The new model is a hybrid variant of the C5 and is the third electrified OMODA available in South Africa. It is…
items.content_html:
OMODA South Africa has added the C5 SHS to its local vehicle range. The new model is a hybrid variant of the C5 and is the third electrified OMODA available in South Africa. It is also the first conventional hybrid electric vehicle (HEV) in the OMODA and JAECOO lineup, as the brand's other electrified models are plug-in hybrids.

The C5 SHS is powered by a turbocharged 1.5-litre four-cylinder petrol engine paired with an electric motor. Combined output stands at 165 kW and 295 N.m of torque. Drive goes to the front wheels through a Dedicated Hybrid Transmission (DHT). The vehicle is reported to accelerate from 0 to 100 km/h in 7.9 seconds and has a claimed top speed of 175 km/h.

Fuel consumption is rated at 4.9 L/100 km on the WLTP cycle, with CO2 emissions of 111 g/km. A 1.83 kWh lithium iron phosphate battery works alongside a 51-litre fuel tank to give the C5 SHS a claimed range of more than 1,000 km.

Suspension consists of a MacPherson strut setup at the front and a multilink arrangement at the rear. The C5 SHS rides on 18-inch alloy wheels with 215/55 tyres, and disc brakes are fitted at all four corners, with ventilated discs at the front.

Standard equipment includes a power-operated boot lid, automatic windscreen wipers, 60/40 split-folding rear seats, a 50W wireless phone charger, keyless entry with push-button start, roof rails, and power-adjustable side mirrors with auto-fold.

Boot capacity is 372 litres, expanding to 1,072 litres with the rear seats folded. Automatic LED headlights, LED daytime running lights, a sunroof, and red-painted brake calipers are also fitted as standard.

Inside, the C5 SHS has a symmetrical dashboard layout and a bridge-style centre console. Dual 12.3-inch screens are fitted, with the touchscreen supporting Bluetooth, wireless Android Auto, and wireless Apple CarPlay. An eight-speaker Sony sound system, intelligent voice command, multi-colour ambient lighting, and acoustic glass for the front occupants are all included.

Front seats are sports-style units trimmed in black leather, with six-way power adjustment, heating, and ventilation for both the driver and front passenger. Dual-zone climate control and rear air vents are also standard.

The C5 SHS carries a five-star Euro NCAP rating, first achieved by the C5 in 2022. Safety equipment includes seven airbags, ISOFIX child seat anchorages, electronic stability programme, and electronic brake-force distribution. Driver assistance systems include front and rear parking sensors, a 360-degree camera, blind-spot detection, rear cross-traffic alert, and lane change assist.

The C5 SHS is available in four solid colours — Bloodstone Red, Carbon Crystal Black, Khaki White, and Phantom Grey — as well as two dual-tone options pairing Carbon Crystal Black with either Aerial Silver or Khaki White.

The C5 SHS is priced at R479,900, with an introductory price of R469,900 for the first month. The purchase price includes a five-year/75,000 km service plan, a five-year/150,000 km factory warranty, and a 10-year/one-million km engine warranty linked to the first owner. The model is available at OMODA and JAECOO dealerships from 8 April 2026.

items.title: Electric Mobility A Shield Against Global Volatility
items.summary: In the 1970s South Africans faced strict fuel rationing, 80km/h speed limits, and dry weekends where not a drop of petrol could be bought. Conflict in the Middle-East back then was a stark lesson in…
items.content_html:
In the 1970s South Africans faced strict fuel rationing, 80km/h speed limits, and dry weekends where not a drop of petrol could be bought. 

Conflict in the Middle-East back then was a stark lesson in how vulnerable a nation becomes when its transport and logistics are tethered to a foreign fossil fuel supply. Fast forward to 2026, and the geopolitical landscape feels eerily familiar. 

As tensions flare again in the Middle East, the global energy market is once more leaning into unpredictability. The fundamental difference is that today, viable alternatives exist.

The Fragility of the Status Quo

As a net energy importer, South Africa remains dangerously exposed. The country currently imports nearly 80% of its oil. 

When international markets shift, inflation spikes, transport costs soar, and the price of everyday goods rises because the trucks moving them are burning expensive, imported fuel.

The country is arguably more dependent on energy today than it was fifty years ago, and the traditional fuel sources underpinning that dependence are subject to forces entirely beyond its control.

The 18-Gigawatt Silver Lining

Whilst the global oil market remains a wildcard, South Africa's domestic energy landscape is quietly undergoing a revolution. 

By the end of 2025, private sector renewable energy projects had reached a significant 18-gigawatt milestone, with solar accounting for over 12.5 gigawatts of that capacity.

This is fundamentally about energy sovereignty. Every kilowatt-hour generated by a South African solar panel to power an electric vehicle is a rand that remains within our borders rather than flowing abroad to pay for foreign oil. 

The capacity to fuel our own vehicles with domestically generated electricity is well within reach.

Learning from the First Movers

Bold blueprints already exist elsewhere. 

In early 2024, Ethiopia became the first country in the world to ban the importation of fossil fuel-powered vehicles, motivated by the steady drain on its foreign currency reserves. 

By pivoting to domestically sourced electricity, Ethiopia is securing its energy future whilst nurturing a local EV manufacturing base.

In South Africa, the maturity of the EV market and an expanding public charging infrastructure signal that the experimental phase is behind us. Consumers and businesses are already realising the benefits of lower maintenance and operating costs, along with greater long-term financial predictability.

Furthermore, there is a strong argument that the case for EVs runs deeper than fuel price.

The Path Forward

The ongoing conflict in the Middle East represents a systemic risk to supply chains and economic growth across the globe. South Africa's exposure to international energy disruptions is a structural vulnerability that demands a structural response.

The transition to electric mobility offers that response. 

It provides a sustainable way to move people and goods whilst creating the kind of stable, predictable environment that attracts investor confidence. Rapid adoption is the priority. The goal is to generate our own energy security rather than import the instability of foreign markets.

items.title: The case for EVs runs deeper than fuel price
items.summary: Every time fuel prices rise, the conversation around electric vehicles picks up. Search trends spike, showroom enquiries increase, and suddenly everyone has an opinion on whether now is the right time to make the switch.
items.content_html:
Every time fuel prices rise, the conversation around electric vehicles picks up.

Search trends spike, showroom enquiries increase, and suddenly everyone has an opinion on whether now is the right time to make the switch. It is a familiar cycle. What is different this time, according to Volvo Car South Africa, is that the conversation is maturing.

Grant Locke, Managing Director of Volvo Car South Africa, puts it plainly.

"Fuel price increases may start the conversation, but they are not the full story. When you look at the total cost of ownership, the flexibility, and the advancements in battery technology, electric vehicles begin to make a compelling case on their own."

That case, it turns out, has very little to do with the petrol price board outside your nearest garage.

The Maths Behind Electric Ownership

One of the most under-appreciated aspects of EV ownership is what happens after the purchase.

Electric vehicles carry fewer moving parts than their petrol-powered counterparts, which translates into reduced maintenance requirements. Servicing intervals can stretch to every two years, with associated costs that tend to sit well below what traditional vehicle owners are accustomed to paying.

Battery technology has also come a long way. Most manufacturers now back their batteries with warranties of up to eight years or 160,000 kilometres. For anyone eyeing the pre-owned market, battery health certificates are increasingly standard, offering a clear picture of remaining capacity before you commit.

Then there is the charging question, which is where the economics become genuinely interesting. Locke is direct about this.

"Whether it is overnight charging on lower tariffs or making use of solar power, drivers have far more control over their monthly mobility costs." The numbers support this. Charging 50kWh via a public DC fast charger can set you back around R400, delivering roughly 250 to 350 kilometres of range.

The same charge at home during off-peak hours could cost closer to R175. For a commuter covering 60 kilometres a day on weekdays, monthly energy costs could settle around R750, based on average consumption figures and current electricity tariffs.

The new Volvo ES90 arrived in SA in Q1

A Broader Economic Argument

Fuel prices do not only affect what you pay at the pump.

South Africa moves more than 80% of its goods by road, which means diesel price increases ripple outward into food costs, retail prices, and household budgets across the board. The forecourt is just the most visible pressure point.

This broader context is shifting how South Africans think about their vehicles. An EV is a hedge against a volatile global oil market, a way to reduce exposure to currency fluctuations, and for those with solar installations, a meaningful step towards genuine energy independence.

Infrastructure is also catching up. Charging networks are expanding, and policy frameworks are gradually aligning with the direction of travel.

Locke sees this shift as something more fundamental than a reaction to short-term price pressures. "As more South Africans begin to understand these dynamics, the shift to electric becomes less about reacting to fuel prices and more about making a smarter long-term mobility choice."

The upfront cost of electric vehicles has historically given buyers pause. That calculation is changing.

Running costs are lower, servicing is simpler, and the technology is more reliable than many assume. For South African drivers willing to look past the initial price tag, the long-term picture is becoming increasingly difficult to ignore.

items.title: SA’s Automotive Future Hinges on Local Battery Manufacturing
items.summary: A new World Bank report suggests that battery manufacturing and local beneficiation of battery minerals will determine whether South Africa can preserve its automotive base and build new industrial capability. The country’s vehicle production for…
items.content_html:
A new World Bank report suggests that battery manufacturing and local beneficiation of battery minerals will determine whether South Africa can preserve its automotive base and build new industrial capability.

The country’s vehicle production for export has long depended on Europe and the United Kingdom. Those markets will gradually phase out internal combustion engine vehicles, which means original equipment manufacturers in South Africa will face shrinking demand for traditional models. 

A strategy that focuses only on final vehicle assembly will not protect local jobs. But a strategy that integrates battery manufacturing, mineral processing and electric vehicle components most likely will.

GDP Boost

The report shows that an integrated battery value chain could add up to R43.6 billion to South Africa’s gross domestic product each year and support more than 58 000 direct and indirect jobs. 

These gains come from activities that range from mineral refining to precursor production to battery pack assembly and electric vehicle components. But expansion in these segments depends on policy certainty, industrial incentives and a clear national position on electric vehicle production.

South Africa’s mineral endowment places it in a strong position. About 70 percent of the world’s identified manganese resources sit in the Northern Cape and most global demand for high‑purity manganese sulphate used in battery cathodes will outstrip supply well before 2035. 

Growing global concern around mineral security creates an opening for South Africa to move beyond ore exports. Investment in battery‑grade processing for manganese, nickel and vanadium could shift value addition into local industry. 

Component manufacturers stand to benefit from greater domestic availability of battery materials, and new entrants could emerge around battery housing, cooling systems, wiring, charging components and safety systems.

No Time To Wait

There is, however, a second message running through the report: we haven’t got much time. Countries like Morocco and Thailand have already aligned their industrial and trade incentives with electric vehicle production. Morocco secured a critical minerals agreement with the United States that gives its producers access to benefits under the US Inflation Reduction Act. 

Thailand has restructured incentives to attract global electric vehicle investment and is positioned as the regional base for several international manufacturers. South Africa still holds a competitive position, but others are moving faster.

Government’s Electric Vehicle White Paper sets out more than 30 recommendations to support electric vehicle assembly, charging networks and component manufacturing. The revised Automotive Investment Scheme offers grants and accelerated depreciation to encourage investment in electric vehicle assembly and parts. 

The updated Automotive Production and Development Programme now includes electric vehicles in its structure. These steps help, but adjustments are still needed, particularly in tariff treatment and localisation incentives.

Import tariffs on electric vehicles remain higher than tariffs on internal combustion engine vehicles in some categories, which raises consumer costs and limits early local demand. A modest reduction in these tariffs would allow more affordable models to enter the market and stimulate demand that supports local assembly. 

Charge point operators are ready to expand networks, but need a national framework, faster municipal approvals and clarity on electricity tariffs for public charging.

In The Zone

South Africa’s industrial zones also require attention. While special economic zones are important tools for attracting battery and electric vehicle investment, many operate unevenly. Duty relief, energy access and customs processes differ from zone to zone, which creates uncertainty for investors. 

A focused effort to position a few zones as dedicated battery and electric vehicle clusters could give South Africa a more compelling investment proposition.

Furthermore, modern battery manufacturing requires technicians, artisans, process controllers and engineers with experience in chemistry, power electronics and quality control. While South Africa has a strong education and training network, few accredited programmes focus specifically on battery systems. 

Partnerships between industry, technical colleges and universities could develop curricula and apprenticeships that prepare workers for these new roles. Local training capacity will also help South African companies meet the environmental and safety standards required for exporting electric vehicle components.

Making The Link

A strong message in the report is that industrial opportunity and environmental responsibility are linked. International buyers expect clear environmental, social and governance standards in mineral sourcing and manufacturing. Therefore, refineries and battery plants that use clean power, water‑efficient processes and traceable supply chains will be more competitive. 

South Africa’s planned expansion of renewable energy could also support green industrial zones where manufacturers are able to show low‑carbon credentials to export markets. The country’s automotive industry has the skills, supplier networks and manufacturing expertise to succeed in the electric era. 

What it needs is a firm industrial direction that links upstream mineral wealth to downstream vehicle production. Battery manufacturing must not be seen as a side project. Instead, it must be seen as the anchor of future automotive competitiveness and the lever that ensures South Africa remains part of shifting global supply chains.

A clear national stance on electric vehicles, combined with smart incentives and regional coordination, can lift the sector and support investment at every level of the value chain. 

Ultimately, South Africa requires decisive action in order to protect a cornerstone of its economy and position it for future growth.

items.title: Why SA Imports Battery Cells
items.summary: South Africa is the world's number one producer of manganese, outputting around 7.4 million tonnes in 2024. This is roughly the same amount as Gabon and Australia combined. The country also holds significant vanadium reserves, with…
items.content_html:
South Africa is the world's number one producer of manganese, outputting around 7.4 million tonnes in 2024. This is roughly the same amount as Gabon and Australia combined. 

The country also holds significant vanadium reserves, with Bushveld Vanadium operating one of the most advanced vanadium electrolyte production facilities in the world. 

Furthermore, the broader Southern African Development Community (SADC) region contains virtually every mineral needed to manufacture a modern battery cell: lithium (Zimbabwe), cobalt (DRC), copper (Zambia), graphite (Mozambique), nickel, iron, phosphorus, and aluminium.

Yet, according to the Faraday Institution's landmark "Charging Ahead" report (March 2026), South Africa does not manufacture a single Li-ion battery cell locally. Every cell in every electric vehicle (EV), every solar backup system, and every grid-scale storage installation is imported, overwhelmingly from China.

This is the Mineral Paradox.

The Numbers Tell the Story

Of all the manganese South Africa produces, only approximately 5% is used domestically, processed by the Manganese Metal Company (MMC) at its Mbombela refinery, which has been operational since 1974. 

The remaining 95% is exported as raw ore or basic metal, with the value-added processing — and the jobs and revenue that come with it — happening elsewhere. The situation with vanadium is similar. Bushveld Vanadium exports the majority of its production, with the Netherlands being the primary destination. 

Between 2019 and 2023, vanadium exports to the Netherlands grew by approximately 56%. South Africa ships the raw material; Europe captures the value. Meanwhile, South Africa's Li-ion battery import bill has grown dramatically. 

Import data from Trade Map shows that China dominates the supply of lithium cells and battery packs into South Africa, with the value of imports rising sharply from 2019 to 2023 as demand for solar storage and EVs accelerated.

Why Hasn't South Africa Closed the Gap?

The report identifies several structural barriers:

Refining capacity is the missing link. Mining the ore is one thing; processing it into battery-grade chemicals (manganese sulfate, vanadium pentoxide, lithium hydroxide) is an entirely different industrial capability. South Africa currently lacks the refining infrastructure to convert its raw mineral wealth into the high-purity precursor materials that cell manufacturers require.

Cell manufacturing requires massive scale. China produces approximately 85% of the world's battery cells and storage systems. The capital investment, supply chain depth, and economies of scale required to compete are enormous. South Africa cannot simply "build a factory" and expect to be competitive overnight.

Policy has not yet aligned with opportunity. Unlike Zimbabwe, which in 2022 banned the export of unprocessed lithium ore to force local beneficiation, South Africa has not yet implemented equivalent "beneficiation mandates" for manganese or vanadium. The minerals leave the country before the value is added.

The skills pipeline is thin at the top. While South Africa has strong graduate-level mining and engineering talent, the report notes a "crippling shortage" of Masters and PhD-level specialists in battery chemistry, materials science, and electrochemical engineering — the exact skills needed to design and operate a cell manufacturing facility.

The Green Shoots: What Is Happening

The picture is not entirely bleak. The report highlights several encouraging developments that suggest the gap is beginning to close:

MMC's new plant: The Manganese Metal Company is currently constructing a 6,000 tonne-per-annum Phase 1 plant to manufacture high-purity manganese sulfate monohydrate — a direct battery precursor material. This is a significant step from raw ore to battery-grade chemical.

Bushveld's electrolyte facility: Bushveld Vanadium's electrolyte production plant in East London has an initial target capacity of 8 million litres per year, with potential expansion to 32 million litres. This positions South Africa as a potential global supplier of vanadium electrolyte for flow batteries.

Local pack assembly is growing. While cell manufacturing remains absent, approximately 10 South African companies — including iG3N, Blue Nova, Freedom Won, Solar MD, and REVOV — are assembling Li-ion battery packs for stationary storage and EV applications. This is midstream activity, not upstream cell production, but it represents a growing local capability.

Mintek's pilot programme: The state-owned research organisation Mintek is running a pilot-scale demonstration of recovery and production of battery-grade salts of nickel, manganese, and copper, as well as battery-grade purified phosphoric acid — the building blocks of a future local cell manufacturing industry.

What the 2026 Roadmap Says

The Charging Ahead report's roadmap for South Africa is explicit: local cell manufacturing must be a long-term national strategic goal, not an afterthought. 

The short-term priority (2025–2030) is to develop battery-grade chemicals and precursors — the refining step that currently sends value offshore. The mid-term goal (2030–2035) is to build at least 10 GWh of local Li-ion cell manufacturing capacity.

For context, 10 GWh is modest by global standards — Tesla's Nevada Gigafactory produces over 35 GWh per year — but it would be transformative for South Africa's industrial base and could create up to 60,000 new jobs across the battery value chain.

The report also notes a strategic decision that South Africa must make in collaboration with its SADC neighbours: which minerals to refine locally, and which to refine at source. 

For manganese, aluminium, and nickel, local refining already makes economic sense. For lithium (from Zimbabwe), cobalt (from the DRC), and graphite (from Mozambique and Madagascar), refining at the source country may be more viable — or South Africa could explore synthetic graphite production from its abundant coal reserves.

What This All Means 

For South African businesses investing in EVs and charging infrastructure today, the Mineral Paradox has a direct practical implication: you are entirely dependent on imported battery technology, with all the currency risk, supply chain vulnerability, and price volatility that entails.

The good news is that this is a solvable problem — and South Africa has more of the raw ingredients to solve it than almost any other country on earth. 

The question is whether policy, industrial investment, and skills development can align fast enough to capture the opportunity before it moves elsewhere.

Disclaimer: This article is based on the South Africa chapter of the Faraday Institution's "Charging Ahead: Battery Ecosystems, Energy Transitions and Electrification in Emerging Economies" report (March 2026). 

The country-specific findings reflect the perspective of the appointed Battery Ambassador, Dr Nithyadharseni Palaniyandy of the University of South Africa (UNISA), and represent a landscape assessment rather than independently verified data or official government policy.

items.title: Off-Grid Ambition Meets the Reality of Fleet Electrification
items.summary: South Africa's fleet electrification conversation has, for several years, been dominated by questions about vehicle availability and purchase cost. Less examined is the infrastructure problem sitting underneath both of those concerns. Even if fleet operators…
items.content_html:
South Africa's fleet electrification conversation has, for several years, been dominated by questions about vehicle availability and purchase cost. 

Less examined is the infrastructure problem sitting underneath both of those concerns. Even if fleet operators were ready to electrify tomorrow, the national electricity grid almost certainly could not support them.

That view is the founding premise of Zero Carbon Charge, a company established in 2022 by Joubert Roux, a Western Cape entrepreneur with a background in service stations, car dealerships and renewable energy development. 

Roux's argument is straightforward. A single town on a national freight corridor, handling a thousand commercial vehicles per day, would generate a five-megawatt electricity demand from charging alone. The distribution network was not designed for anything close to that. 

His response has been to build a network of fully off-grid charging stations powered entirely by on-site solar generation and battery storage, with backup generators for redundancy. 

The first proof-of-concept site, at Voëlfontein in the Northern Cape, has been running for 14 months. Two commercial sites on the N3 are under construction, with equipment already in Durban Harbour and a go-live date of mid-May. A logistics integrator, ZIMI, has contracted to consume 50% of available energy across both sites from day one.

The N3 project will be the first real test of whether the model works commercially, not just technically.

The Economics Are Tighter Than They Look

Zero Carbon Charge prices its DC fast charging at R9 per kilowatt-hour, fixed annually. Each site costs approximately R16.5 million to build in its first phase, with a further R6 million required to double capacity. Roux says the break-even threshold is four charging sessions per day, with profitability reached at seven.

Those numbers are low enough to raise questions about utilisation risk, particularly given that South Africa's EV fleet remains small. Roux acknowledges the challenge but points to the ZIMI off-take agreement as a model for de-risking early-stage sites through contracted commercial demand. 

The sites are also designed to be modular, allowing solar, battery and charging capacity to be scaled incrementally as usage data accumulates rather than requiring operators to commit to full build-out upfront. The fixed-price energy model is likely to attract attention from fleet operators who have spent years absorbing diesel price volatility. 

Whether that interest translates into contracted off-take agreements at the pace Zero Carbon Charge needs remains to be seen. The company's financial viability depends on replicating the ZIMI structure across a significant portion of its planned 120-site network.

"You can control your input costs, so you can control your output costs," Roux adds. That argument becomes considerably more compelling as diesel prices rise. It becomes less compelling if site utilisation lags behind projections in the early years of the rollout.

Commercial Vehicles Change the Scale Entirely

The passenger vehicle market is not where the infrastructure challenge becomes acute. It is in commercial transport, where a single heavy vehicle consumes the energy equivalent of approximately nine passenger EVs per charge cycle. At that scale, the grid constraint Roux identified four years ago is not a future problem. It is a present one.

Zero Carbon Charge has secured sites capable of supporting 50 to 100-megawatt generation setups for dedicated truck charging hubs, and the company is in early discussions about cross-border corridor electrification, including the Gaborone-Durban route and the broader Trans-Kalahari corridor spanning Mozambique, South Africa, Botswana and Namibia. 

Roux estimates that 638 charging stations would be sufficient to electrify the major freight corridors across the continent. That figure is worth sitting with. It implies a capital requirement running into the tens of billions of rands, across multiple regulatory jurisdictions, with no guarantee of the policy support that would make the economics work at that scale. 

Roux is candid about the regulatory friction the company has already encountered in South Africa, including approval processes that have stretched beyond 1,000 days at some sites, and a proposed SANRAL rest and service facility policy that he argues would effectively tax private infrastructure development along national roads.

"The national roads should be a driver of development," he says. "Not an impediment."

What the Tipping Point Requires

Fleet managers watching this space will be aware that EV adoption in South Africa has so far been concentrated in the passenger vehicle segment, and even there, penetration remains low. The arrival of more affordable vehicles in the R300,000 to R400,000 range, led by Chinese manufacturers including BYD, has begun to shift the calculus. 

Plug-in hybrid sales are growing rapidly, and data from Mercedes-Benz suggests that once a market reaches 5% EV penetration of new vehicle sales, it tends to climb to 15% within three years. South Africa has not reached that threshold yet. Getting there will require both affordable vehicles and reliable charging infrastructure to be present simultaneously. 

Zero Carbon Charge is building one side of that equation. The other side depends on import duty policy, OEM investment decisions and the pace at which fleet operators are willing to commit capital to vehicle transition.

The N3 corridor going live in May will not resolve those questions. It will, however, provide the first substantive evidence of whether off-grid charging infrastructure can operate commercially at the scale the country's fleet electrification ambitions require.

That evidence is long overdue.

Zero Carbon Charge's N3 charging stations are scheduled to go live in mid-May. The company's N1 corridor rollout, connecting Cape Town to Musina, is targeted for completion by the end of the year. -Ed.

items.title: Petrol, diesel or PHEV: which makes financial sense?
items.summary: Global oil markets have been unsettled by ongoing tensions between Iran and the United States, and the effects are reaching South African forecourts. The threat to supply routes through the Strait of Hormuz has kept…
items.content_html:
Global oil markets have been unsettled by ongoing tensions between Iran and the United States, and the effects are reaching South African forecourts. The threat to supply routes through the Strait of Hormuz has kept energy traders on edge, and that pressure is now translating into real costs for local motorists.

Forecourt pressure mounts

Brent crude has moved sharply on each new development in the region. Data from the Central Energy Fund has pointed to the risk of a significant fuel-price increase in April, with some grades potentially moving beyond R25 per litre if current conditions persist. Gauteng 95 ULP already sits at R20.30 per litre, leaving motorists with little buffer against further increases.

For most vehicle owners, the monthly repayment is only part of the financial picture. The more pressing question is what a vehicle costs to run week to week, and that is where powertrain choice starts to matter more than it might in a stable fuel-price environment.

Comparing the cost per kilometre

To put some numbers to that question, three family SUVs were compared using their quoted combined fuel-consumption figures and current Gauteng pump prices. The vehicles selected were the Toyota RAV4 2.0 GX-R AWD running on petrol, the Hyundai Tucson 2.0D Executive on diesel, and the JAECOO J7 SHS, a plug-in hybrid.

The RAV4 returns a quoted 6.7 litres per 100 km. At R20.19 per litre for 95 ULP, that works out to approximately R135.27 per 100 km. The Tucson is rated at 7.4 litres per 100 km, and at R18.60 per litre for diesel, the cost comes to roughly R137.64 per 100 km.

The two are closer than many buyers might expect, given that diesel has traditionally been seen as the more economical option for higher-mileage use.The J7 SHS returns a quoted combined figure of 4.7 litres per 100 km, dropping to a manufacturer-claimed 1.0 litre per 100 km when the battery is fully charged.

Using the 4.7-litre figure, the running cost works out to approximately R94.89 per 100 km, which is around R40 less than the RAV4 and R43 less than the Tucson over the same distance. Scaled up to a Johannesburg to Durban return trip, the JAECOO saves roughly R457 against the Toyota and approximately R484 against the Hyundai on fuel alone.

Why PHEVs are gaining ground

Those figures illustrate why plug-in hybrids are attracting more attention as pump prices rise. They occupy a practical middle ground, offering lower fuel consumption than a conventional internal-combustion vehicle without the dependency on public charging infrastructure that comes with a fully electric car.

For buyers in areas where charging networks remain patchy, that is a meaningful consideration. The efficiency advantage of a PHEV is, however, conditional. The savings depend on the battery being charged consistently and regularly. When that happens, the fuel-consumption figures become achievable in everyday driving.

When it does not, the vehicle effectively operates as a heavier petrol car, and the consumption figures shift accordingly. Electricity costs also need to be factored into the total running-cost calculation, even if they are generally lower than the equivalent fuel spend.

What the comparison does show is that the gap between petrol and diesel running costs has narrowed considerably at current price levels. The more meaningful gap now sits between conventional powertrains and electrified options.

As fuel prices remain under pressure, that gap is likely to become a more prominent factor in purchasing decisions, particularly for buyers covering significant distances each month.

items.title: CNBM and Geely SA Drive Clean Energy Forward
items.summary: China National Building Materials (CNBM) South Africa and Geely Auto South Africa have signed a Strategic Cooperation Framework Agreement to work together on clean energy and electric mobility projects across South Africa and the wider…
items.content_html:
China National Building Materials (CNBM) South Africa and Geely Auto South Africa have signed a Strategic Cooperation Framework Agreement to work together on clean energy and electric mobility projects across South Africa and the wider Southern African region. The agreement was signed at the Future Energy Storage Exhibition in Johannesburg on 25 March 2026.

What the partnership is about

CNBM South Africa specialises in solar power and battery energy storage systems. Geely Auto South Africa focuses on electric vehicles (EVs) and vehicle-to-grid (V2G) technology, which allows electric cars to feed power back into the electricity grid.

Together, the two companies will develop an integrated network of clean energy solutions, covering solar generation, battery storage, EV charging infrastructure and green supply chains. The goal is to make clean energy more accessible and practical for South African consumers and businesses.

Why this matters for South Africa

South Africa is working to reduce its reliance on fossil fuels and build a more reliable, sustainable energy system. This partnership directly supports those goals by bringing together two complementary sets of expertise under one shared vision.

The collaboration also supports local industrial development. Both companies are committed to building localised supply chains, which means more goods and services will be sourced and produced within South Africa. This creates opportunities for local businesses and helps develop skills within the country.

What they will build together

Under the agreement, CNBM South Africa and Geely South Africa will work together across several key areas.They will coordinate the rollout of new energy vehicles alongside the charging infrastructure needed to support them.

They will also implement solar-storage-charging solutions, where solar panels, battery storage and EV chargers work together as a single system. In addition, both companies will focus on developing local supply chains to support long-term growth and sustainability.

The initial focus will be on South Africa, with plans to expand across Southern Africa as the partnership develops.

Voices from the partnership

"This agreement marks an important step in our mission to bring practical, integrated clean energy solutions to South Africa," said a spokesperson for CNBM South Africa. "By working with Geely South Africa, we can offer a much more complete solution to our customers and partners."

A spokesperson for Geely Auto South Africa added, "Electric vehicles are only part of the answer. When you combine them with solar power and smart storage, you create something far more powerful. That is exactly what this partnership makes possible."

A shared commitment to sustainability

Both companies have expressed a strong commitment to environmental and social responsibility. The partnership is designed not only to deliver commercial results but also to contribute meaningfully to South Africa's sustainable development.

CNBM South Africa has been active in the South African market for a number of years, building expertise in green energy technologies and developing strong relationships with local partners. Geely Auto South Africa brings global experience in electric mobility and a growing presence in the South African automotive market.

CNBM South Africa and Geely South Africa will begin practical collaboration in the coming months, with joint projects expected to be announced as the partnership progresses. Both companies see South Africa as a key market and are committed to playing a long-term role in the country's clean energy future.
